dialogue.utterance: 911 units to 321 Woodchuck Drive for a sick adult male. ST-3 on scene now.
Hi sir, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've had nausea and vomiting most of the day. It got worse a little while ago.
Would you please state your full name and today's date?
My name is James. Today is March 9th.
Mr. Smith, can you follow my finger with your eyes?
What city are we in, sir?
On command, can you raise your left hand?
Patient is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. (aox4).
Sir, are you having any trouble breathing or airway discomfort?
Respiration rate is 16, unlabored with normal depth and no cyanosis.
Pulse is currently 113, strong and regular. Blood pressure is 156 over 96. No signs of diaphoresis.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me a bit more about these symptoms and when they got worse?
I began feeling sick this morning after breakfast. It's definitely gotten worse since then.
Have you experienced blood in your vomit?
No, none that I remember.
Any other symptoms accompanying the nausea and vomiting?
A mild headache and some uncharacteristic tremors in my hands. The nausea is about a 2 out of 10. (pain-pain-2).
Do you have a history of heart conditions or surgeries?
Yes, I have congestive heart failure.
What medications are you currently taking, sir?
Just Lasix.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Did anything unusual happen leading to this, like a specific meal or activity?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No, it just started on its own.
Are you currently pale or diaphoretic?
No, I don't feel that way at all.
Pulse is 113, regular in rhythm and of strong quality.
Respiratory rate is 16, consistent in depth and no apparent distress.
Blood pressure is 155 over 105. Remaining within anticipated range for this presentation.
EKG reading reveals sinus tachycardia with premature atrial and junctional contractions (PACs and PJC). (pac_s, pjc_s).
Oxygen saturation is 97%, consistent with breathing room air.
The blood glucose is reading abnormally low at 0. We'll take repeated readings.
Your Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is 15, which means you're alert and oriented (aox4).
We will now start an IV on your arm to provide fluids and deliver medications.
IV successfully placed in your left forearm, 20-gauge catheter.
Giving a 300 cc bolus of normal saline to correct for initial fluid loss.
Starting four lead ECG monitoring as part of field protocol.
We'll also give you 4 mg of Zofran intravenously to manage the nausea.
Per protocol 3-9, we'll continue hydration and monitor for transport.
Pulse has increased to 134. Still regular and strong.
Breathing remains unlabored and within normal range at 15 per minute.
Your oxygen saturation has slightly improved to 98%.
EKG monitoring remains stable with no significant changes in rhythm.
Which hospital would you like us to transport you to, sir?
Please take me to McGuire. That's where I usually go.
We'll assist you onto the stretcher now. Is that okay with you?
Mr. Smith is now safely loaded and secured using side rails and shoulder straps.
The patient is being transferred to Emergency Department room 1. (ed_rm).
All equipment and medications have been accounted for, and we are exiting the scene.
Any recent history of pain or urgency with urination, sir?
I haven't gone, and I don't feel strong about this now.
Pupils round, equal, and reactive to light.
Any chest pain or discomfort, sir?
No chest pain today, just the nausea.
No distension or palpable masses on exam.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No drainage, foreign bodies, or signs of obstruction.
No deformity or angulation, but patient reports tremors not typical for them.
Continuing to monitor for signs of hypoglycemia with no current changes noted.
No recent movement or signs of discomfort during positioning.
Confirmed neckline upright at all times, all movement controlled.
Vital signs stable, repeatable and consistent since start of assessment.
No signs of poor peripheral perfusion; skin warm and dry at this time.
Rechecking IV site shows no infiltration or complications.
Based on protocol and patient dynamics, no other interventions under medical management needed at this time.
Nausea onset is this morning, worsening in the past hour.
Nothing known to trigger or worsen symptoms.
Described as an ongoing dull sensation, not sharp or crampy.
No radiation of sensation noted or reported.
Pain remains at 2 out of 10 per patient self-report. (pain-pain-2)
Duration of nausea since 8 a.m., with four episodes of vomiting.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - signs and symptoms
dialogue.utterance: No new signs or symptoms reported during reassessment.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - pertinent past medical history
dialogue.utterance: CHF controlled with current regimen.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - medications
dialogue.utterance: The patient still states he is only on Lasix.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No specific events occurred before this episode.
Monitoring ongoing and has remained consistent with our field diagnosis.
Patient safe for transport with no signs of acute deterioration at this time.
Continuously monitoring for changes during transport.
Mr. Smith currently denies lightheadedness or other concerns.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - last oral intake
dialogue.utterance: Patient has had no oral intake all day.

dialogue.micro_intent: redo history of present illness - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No specific events occurred before this episode.
Patient stable en route to VAMC Richmond (McGuire), cold load ready.
Patient currently incident free and awaiting ED physician room availability.
Additional fluids administered and Zofran given as part of field treatment.
No further changes anticipated during transport unless patient describes new symptoms.
Regular provider advancing along with stretcher at all times for safety.
No new trends recorded.
No desaturation or oxygen therapy required for patient thus far.
Pre-arranged with hospital, no immediate alternate requested.
No equipment issues or malfunctions during transport observed to date.
Ensuring stretcher is locked and immobilized per protocol.
No movement or unexpected activity reported during all time points.
All documentation and ticketing status is completed and transferable to ED records.
Ambulance now ready for transport, route and destination confirmed.
Emergency lights and siren not in use at this time.
Patient currently quiet, no acute distress and no interruptions.
All medications administered according to hospital standing order.
No immediate interventions required en route based on current condition.
ED been contacted for arrival time and access.
Preparing for hospital arrival now.
Patient will be handed directly to ED staff of room 1 for processing.
Emergency department is aware of patient condition and JFK tag utilized.
